steps: List every AI automation currently running at your station, including any nobody remembers setting up.
For each one, list every tool it can actually reach — not what its instructions say, what it is physically able to do: send, delete, post, read.
Strip out every tool it doesn't need for its actual job, especially any send, delete, or publish access it isn't using.
For anything that can reach your CRM, your website, or your listener email list, add a hard restriction at the tool level — not just a written instruction telling it not to.
Ask Claude to help you list the edge cases each automation could hit and what a safe failure looks like for each one.
Check each automation's actual output from the last two weeks against what it's supposed to be doing.
Put a date on the calendar to repeat this whole check, so a new automation can't quietly reappear unwatched.
verify: For each automation, look at the tools it still has and try to name one bad real-world action it could take with each — if you can name one, remove that tool now, don't wait for it to happen.
